messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe the research methodology, technical approach, risk mitigation strategies, and TRL progression.
Write the Methodology and approach section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

1.3 Concept and methodology; quality of the measures  
1.3.1 Concept and approach  
AI-CUBE concept  
Our AI-CUBE concept – as visualised more in detail in Figure 3 above – is based on a tri-axial mapping of AI and BD technologies in the different process industry sectors and macro-application-process areas. It allows us to map and visualise the STATUS of AI & BD use and penetration per SPIRE sector (cement, ceramics, chemicals, engineering, minerals and ores, non-ferrous metals, steel and water) and per macro-application-process areas (e.g. research and innovation management and planning, process control, supply chain management, predictive maintenance, product customization). These application areas and related AI & BD technologies will be pre-defined based on literature research, including research on previously funded projects, but cyclically updated during our project, when the most promising business cases to be defined through a bottom-up engagement process with industry stakeholders become clear. As confirmed by research, 57% of companies expect AI to have an impact on ‘business areas’ that are entirely unknown to us today, indicating that value drivers, pre-defined macro-application areas and future AI & BD business cases are currently merely indicative. The ‘cube concept’ allows us to set the stage for the development and integration of four sets of key results which are key to drive the opportunity assessment and take-up of AI and BD by the European process industries:  
1. MLA: A novel AI and BD Maturity Assessment Level model based on five activities: inception, elaboration, construction, deployment and maintenance.  
2. The AI-CUBE tool: to be used as a mapping tool by individual industries or the sector to assess their state of advancement in AI and BD, and needs for further development.  
3. A set of inspirational AI and BD Business Cases in the process industries and an AI-CUBE Business Model Game: to be used by industries or the sector, to stimulate reasoning about how AI and BD can change their Business Models and Value Chains for the future, and hence what actions to take.  
4. Roadmaps and Infographics: A set of several roadmaps and easy to consult Infographics, that outline the path towards:  
   a. transferring good AI & BD practices from one SPIRE sector to another  
   b. further developing and taking-up technology solutions towards AI & BD Business Cases of the future for the different SPIRE process industry sectors.  

The AI-CUBE Approach  
Our methodological AI-CUBE approach (see Figure 4) uses the “cube-concept” to assess and visualise the AI and BD STATUS (3.1.) based on literature research and stock-taking with SPIRE industries stakeholders (1.1. and 2.1.). Based on the initial STATUS assessment in the first phase of our project, the definition of future AI and BD business cases in the second phase, resulting from the MAMCA stakeholder engagement process, as well as a detailed gap-analysis, in the third phase a set of actions to realise the targeted AI and BD strategies and roadmaps will be developed (4.3.).  

Figure 4. The AI-CUBE Approach  
Once the AI and BD STATUS has been established, a Maturity Level Assessment (MLA) method and standards will be developed (2.2.) by our AI-CUBE team, and validated with the stakeholders’ group through a series of interviews and Workshops (2.1.) following a Multi-Actor Multi-Criteria Analysis methodology. This methodology (see 1.3.3.1) will be used to obtain a widely supported and consensus-based action plan for the industrial consultation including a broad stakeholder community representing the main industry actors throughout all the SPIRE sectors. This stepwise and scientific approach allows us to involve a large group of stakeholders and interested actors in the process. Our consortium has strong connections with SPIRE industries and will use its support and experience to integrate all the sectors and reach all the involved actors by engaging strong and committed stakeholders. Several large process industries have already expressed their support to our AI-CUBE project (see letters of Support) and will be engaged in the process.  

The Multi-Actor Multi-Criteria Analysis (MAMCA) approach will be used to define the business areas, business cases and models, related organisational core processes and related AI & BD technologies, in relation to where the largest impact of AI and BD technologies is expected by the stakeholder communities, as well as gap analysis and validation of development requirements. Based on an initial stock-taking and status assessment (the “CUBE”), together with industry stakeholders, the most promising AI and BD Business Cases of the future (4.1.) will be developed, taking into account both industrial competitiveness and sustainability objectives (SPIRE 2050 and Green Deal). A Business Model Innovation Game, developed and validated within the previous INSPIRE CSA, will be adapted for AI-CUBE to engage the industry stakeholders through a “serious game approach”, to define the impact of AI and BD on their process industries. Using the CUBE, promising business cases and models will be mapped against STATUS (3.1) and a gap analysis will be carried out (4.1.). This will allow us to identify AI and BD development areas, as well as those solutions for which good practices already exist in other SPIRE process industries, which can be adapted and transferred (3.2.). Methods to transfer and better exploit AI and BD will be defined (3.3.), and the impact (3.3.) of these potential developments will be assessed together with stakeholders through a series of Workshops with stakeholders (2.1.). The promising AI and BD Business Cases for the future will be further analysed through case-studies and business models (4.2.) and will be discussed with the stakeholder community, as input for the final results: the development of individual roadmaps for each SPIRE process sector to progress towards these targeted AI and BD business cases.  

Finally, to foster dissemination and take-up of results, we will design easy to use and digest AI and BD Infographics, to help realise one of the key final challenges of CSA type projects, to transfer the acquired knowledge and tools towards the wider (process industry) stakeholder community. Our AI-CUBE roadmaps will investigate:  
- How to identify the major business problems/opportunities and develop AI business strategies, based on the long-term strategic issues faced by the process industry in Europe?  
- What type of AI to deploy, given the uncertainty regarding which technology will become widespread?  
- How to make use of the lessons learned from the implementation of AI solutions so far (most likely in technology-focused firms) in the process industry?  
- What would be the major/particular challenges that the process industry would face in deploying AI?  

We will analyse success stories and failures in similar settings and industrial applications (e.g., targeted marketing, dynamic pricing, manufacturing automation) to answer the question: How to manage AI related risks? What kind of new technologies should be coupled with AI in sectors with heavy assets and long production cycle times, such as some sectors in the process industry? The purpose is to identify the potential of such technologies (e.g., machine and deep learning, image recognition) in facilitating the implementation of the recommendations from AI solutions for achieving meaningful impact on different value chain functions (i.e., design, innovation, sourcing, making, storage, delivery, marketing, sales, use, after-sales, recycling/remanufacturing). This would help decision makers in the process industry to take full advantage of AI, to close the “AI gap” and not to fall behind technology-focused industries.  

- What are the available methods/tools/processes to acquire relevant data?  
- How can we in the process industry establish processes to identify the type of information necessary to improve financial/operational/environmental performance and how AI can contribute to the collection and analysis of such data/information?  
- How to increase the involvement of data science in the decision-making process at higher levels?  
- How to scale deployment of AI technologies across industries/locations?  
- What kind of AI ecosystems need to be established (e.g., IoT infrastructure, IT expertise, top AI talent)?  
- How would this benefit not only single firms, but also value chains or industrial parks (e.g., industrial symbiosis, supply chain coordination)?  
- How to guarantee that the process industry workforce is adequately trained in applying and benefiting from the use of data-driven technologies, such as AI and BD?  
- How to recruit employees with the proper digital skill set?  
- How to determine the tasks/decisions that need to be automated?  
- What would be the process governing the human-machine interaction (e.g., how would the recommendations from the AI solutions be handled, under what conditions would the human decision maker accept or reject the decision proposed by the AI solution)?  
- What would be the impact of the deployment of such AI technologies and BD on different stakeholders in a value chain?  
- Would some players (e.g., freight forwarder or a distributor in a supply chain, humans in individual firms) run the risk of being disintermediated because of AI solutions?  
- Do the AI solutions have the potential to create all-win solutions?  

1.3.2 State of the art  
1.3.2.1 State of art: Data driven technologies  
In recent years, digitalization has become more and more important, so that it has a high significance in the manufacturing and planning processes of the process industry. Europe is indeed the third country at world level for what concerns AI players and investments (see fig. 5) with a growing shift of investments both from the public and private sector. In 5 years from now (see fig. 6), the market is expected to grow almost ten times

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe the research methodology, technical approach, risk mitigation strategies, and TRL progression.
Write the Methodology and approach section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

1.3 Concept and methodology  
Overall concept  
Today resource-efficient plastics are present in an infinite range of products and applications helping us to save energy, carbon dioxide emissions, water and even food. They contribute to circularity, to health and safety and to mitigate climate change. However, to make the most of these materials, challenges related to the EoL of certain products, and particularly plastic packaging, still need to be addressed. The increasing use of biopolymers in packaging applications is aligned with this necessity, when alternative EoL to landfilling, incineration or mechanical recycling are considered, like biodegradability or compostability. But bioplastics are not only used for this purpose, but others like the use of non-fossil based raw materials. Linking both purposes and considering the fact that in Europe we still landfill nearly 40-50% of plastic waste and only 25-31% is currently recycled, bioplastics are a good alternative to conventional polymers, covering our current EU policies in terms of the use of non-fossil based resources, the recyclability rate increase of materials and packaging products (when using non-biodegradable/compostable bioplastics) or the look for the most favourable EoL according to the final application.  
Our BioSupPack project addresses these EU policies through the use of brewery waste, available in large amounts and with a suitable composition for the production of PHA based bioplastics in high yield fermentation processes, but also goes a step further when considering EoL of the new biobased packaging, as it will boost recyclability of the biobased materials and products, by enzymatic recycling of the packaging waste. This EoL allows us to get more value from the packaging materials compared to biodegradability or compostability, as precursors suitable for feeding the fermentation processes or even polymerizing PHA, increasing circularity. In addition, our BioSupPack project addresses also the specific need for recyclability of current single use packaging (service packaging) made of PE coated paper/cardboard, difficult to recycle due to the complex separation of both materials, by the use of PHA based coatings and the use of enzymes that degrade then after the packaging life, leaving the paper/cardboard 100% available for recyclability. Being paper and cardboard the packaging material with the highest recycling rate in Europe (85.8% across the 28 EU countries), our project will allow integrating this waste stream in the recycling loop.  
Our BioSupPack project will:  
1. Develop and produce at a demonstrative level a range of bio-based material formulations with tailored properties for identified packaging applications, derived from available agri-food by-products (PHA/PHB based compounds) and from commercially available PHAs (wet coatings).  
2. Validate the use of the resulting biobased materials in several foods, cosmetic, homecare & beverage packaging and their environmental impacts and economic feasibility compared with existing benchmarks.  
3. Provide recommendations for suitable business approaches for the new packaging materials and derivatives from BSG, from an integrated bio-based value chain perspective including, among others, consumers & end users requesting more sustainable solutions, feedstock logistics and management and waste management bodies through a series of outreach activities exchanging knowledge between the relevant actors.  
The considered technologies are summarised in Table 2, together with their purpose.

Increase efficiency and sustainability in enzymatic recycling as EoL, with carbon source recycling and no chemical usage for PHA recycling, causing no damage to the environment. In our BioSupPack project, our trans-disciplinary team, formed by 17 partners from 8 countries across Europe, combines all the relevant skills and organisations needed to address the full extent of the challenge of upscaling the production of innovative sustainable bio-based compounds and coatings in plastic packaging solutions. We have a very strong consortium with mature suppliers pioneering bioplastic & packaging converting companies, innovative technology companies and RTDs, as well as SMEs and large end users. Our participation of leading industry players proves the actual soundness of the concept to generate large impact and will maximize our consortium capability in creating and accelerating the market uptake of the new biobased materials and packaging. The participation of key stakeholders with specific expertise involved in an Advisory Board (AB) will provide high value to our project results and impacts, collaborating in different points of the value chain (details in section 4.5 and support letters annexed to section 5).  
Positioning of our project  
Most of our BioSupPack activities will be implemented starting at TRL5, and even some of them, like plasma technology for improving packaging performance or PHA based compounds production, start with a more advanced TRL6, with a target of a consolidated TRL7 and TRL8 respectively at the project end, when our technologies will be combined in the final project demonstrators to be produced first at RTDs pilot scale and finally at the industrial facilities of the plastic converters, with the subsequent validation by the end-users in operational environment. In the specific cases of the use of extremophilic bacteria in fermentation processes, the development of squeezable packaging, and the use of plasma technology in both BSG and packaging waste pre-treatment as well as the fermentation routes for PHB production from PHB monomers from recycling, start at TRL4 and they will achieve a TRL6. Proposed innovations are based on prior research from our involved partners for the same or other base biopolymers for packaging applications, and on technologies defined at the pilot level or in other industrial sectors, as Table 3 shows.

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe the research methodology, technical approach, risk mitigation strategies, and TRL progression.
Write the Methodology and approach section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

1.3 Concept and methodology  
1.3.1 Concept  
1.3.1.1 CEM-WAVE overall concept  
The escalating problems resulting from climate change and especially the environmental impacts of anthropogenic activities are getting advisedly exponential attention. This is reflected in the political direction of the recently established European Commission: the Communication called “The European Green Deal” clearly outlines the mid-term and long-term objectives, which can be synthesised in “to transform the EU into a fair and prosperous society, with a modern resource-efficient and competitive economy where there are no net emissions of greenhouse gases in 2050 and where economic growth is decoupled from resource use”. In order to reach those main objectives, several key elements have been identified. Two of them are shared by and at the basis of our project:  
• Mobilising industries for a clean and circular economy: energy-intensive industries, such as steel, chemicals and cement, are indispensable to Europe’s economy, as they supply several key value chains. The decarbonisation and modernisation of this sector are thus essential, as also testified by the recommendations published by the High Level Group of energy-intensive industries, which demonstrates the industry’s commitment to these objectives [1]. Furthermore, EU industry needs ‘climate and resource frontrunners’ to develop the first commercial applications of breakthrough technologies in key industrial sectors by 2030. Priority areas include clean hydrogen, fuel cells and other alternative fuels, energy storage, and carbon capture, storage and utilisation. One of these priorities is towards clean steel breakthrough technologies leading to a zero-carbon steel making process by 2030. The proposal to support zero carbon steelmaking processes by 2030 is one of the actions expected to be taken in 2020.  
• Mobilising research and fostering innovation: new technologies, sustainable solutions and disruptive innovation are critical to achieve the objectives of the European Green Deal; apart from the full range of instruments available under the Horizon Europe programme, partnerships with industry and Member States will support research and innovation on low-carbon steel making among others. In such context, research and innovation are directed in several directions including change of processes which will have to use clean energy sources to get lower CO2 emissions. For energy intensive sectors, where very high temperatures have to be reached (e.g. steelmaking), we will need to follow one or more low-emissions pathways. Concerning the steel industry, the tender will be to use energy inputs provided from circular carbon technologies, fossil fuels with carbon capture and storage (CCS) and clean power for hydrogen-based ironmaking processes. Clean hydrogen is enjoying unprecedented political and business momentum, with the number of policies and projects around the world. Two key developments have contributed to the growth of hydrogen in recent years: the cost of hydrogen supply from renewables has come down and continues to fall, while the urgency of greenhouse gas emission mitigation has increased [2]. The hydrogen debate has evolved over the past two decades, with a shift in attention from applications for the auto industry to hard to-decarbonise sectors such as energy-intensive industries, trucks, aviation, shipping and heating applications. Ensuring a low-carbon, clean hydrogen supply is essential; current and future sourcing options include: fossil fuel-based hydrogen production (grey hydrogen); fossil fuel-based hydrogen production combined with carbon capture, utilisation and storage (CCUS; blue hydrogen); and hydrogen from renewables (green hydrogen). Regardless of the hydrogen source. Greener fuels containing hydrogen (mixtures of hydrogen and bio-methane), that will be used to replace current fuels, can cause harmful phenomena such as hydrogen embrittlement for metals and hydrothermal attack for ceramics. These conditions would not be sustained by the currently used materials. We thus need a novel material that will be able to endure in such future extreme and fluctuating conditions for a long time, and contemporarily maintain its high-performance properties. Our project is based on the concept that a material able to withstand such conditions while keeping its high performances for long times already exists, i.e. Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMCs): CMCs are a class of materials that show considerable promise to provide fracture-toughness values similar to those for metals such as cast iron, yet are much lighter and designed for applications in severe environments (often combining high temperatures, high stress levels and corrosive atmospheres). Thanks to their exceptional properties, including high toughness, low density, high temperature, wear and corrosion resistance, these materials are increasingly being considered for use in applications where reliable operations under extreme conditions are required. Currently they are being introduced into many other new fields and their range of applications will grow if our project’s manufacturing costs will be significantly reduced [3]. Our innovative idea is thus directed at drastically reducing costs of CMC production to allow their sustainable use in sectors where their benefit would be enormous both from the environmental impact and from the productivity points of view. To highlight the impact of our project, one of the most important European stakeholders on the ceramic market (Sacmi) wrote an endorsement letter. Starting from the results of HELM [4] (New technologies based on physical processing of materials for mechanical or electro-technical applications – FP7 – NMP – GA N. 280464), FP7 project in which 5 out of the 13 partners were involved, we aim at advancing Microwave assisted Chemical Vapor Infiltration (MW-CVI) technologies. The use of these technologies will in fact (as described in Fig.1.1):  
• Drastically reduce the time of CMCs processing, which is one of the main components of this product costs;  
• The replacement of magnetrons with newer solid-state sources will grant a fast, precise and automatic tuning of operational frequency and phase with a high coupling level and a much more uniform temperature profile in the preform. Thus, CMCs produced with MW-CVI will be cheaper but, at the same time will have higher performances than the materials currently used.  
In the steelmaking sector, most blast furnaces (BF) and subsequently basic oxygen furnaces (BOF), currently reduce iron ore using a high-temperature synthetic gas derived from coal and coke as energy inputs. Specifically, in steel processing lines, indirect heating of products in protective atmosphere is carried out with radiant tubes equipped with gas-fired burners (around 50 burners per horizontal furnace and 150 burners per vertical furnace). These components are made of different grades of Inconel/stainless steel, depending on the location (first leg or end leg). In our project, we aim at validating the use of non-oxide and oxide CMCs in radiant tube furnaces instead of the currently used material. Our technology will reach TRL 5. Demanding for this concept to be feasible and to find applicable solutions, we planned the research and innovation activities as described below and in the Figure 1.2. Set-up of the materials and of the Microwave technology will be performed (WP1-2); the hearth of our project activity is the oxide and non-oxide CMC production processes (WP3), contemporary material characterisation (WP4), AI-assisted modelling (WP5) and CMCs joining techniques (WP6), all feeding each other in a circular process, will identify the best composition and process parameters for obtaining the best oxide and non-oxide CMC materials. Sustainability aspects, both in terms of market analysis and environmental impacts will be investigated, (LCA, LCC, TA and Risk Analyses - WP8). All these activities will be completed by Dissemination, Communication and Exploitation (WP9) and Coordination and management (WP10) activities. The involvement of several Research and Technology Organisations (RTOs) with diverse expertise at the international level in Materials Science, and past projects in collaboration with material or sectoral industries, will ensure the experience for effective technology transfer from research to industrial applications. On top of the well-defined business case, an exploitation strategy has been carefully planned in order to give our project results larger possibilities of being used in other sectors where similar conditions are expected to be in place. The multi-disciplinary team (Material Science and Engineering, Electronic Engineering, including experts in Electromagnetic wave propagation and their interaction with matter, Physicists, Chemists and Environmental Scientists) brings together European expertise in all the relevant fields required by our project. Between us, the consortium has the skills needed to achieve the project objectives and to develop and transfer the know-how to further enable industrial-scale exploitation, creating the required political and social environment underpinned by economics (business case) that encourages both transfer and adoption of these ideas. Our project will enable this development and transfer of know-how through its value chain of actors. The participating academic and technology institutes (8 RTOs) include both international experts in MW-CVI processing of non-oxide CMCs (UNIPI) and oxide CMCs (UoB), one of the leaders in all process chains of CMCs including fibre and EBC coating (FHF), renowned expert in experimental and numerical electromagnetic methods including high-temperature dielectric characterization of CMC materials (IPCF-CNR), the Italian largest institute on thermo-mechanical and non-destructive testing for CMCs (ENEA), internationally renowned group on micro/macro-scale modelling using AI-aided tools of CMCs (CNRS), and the top research teams on advanced joining and integration technologies for CMCs including testing and monitoring of the joined components (POLITO). The industry participants include the industrial final end-user (AMIII) which is the world’s leading steel and mining company, one of the SME leading German suppliers of microwave ovens for industry and research (FM), a SME specialised in the development of CVD processes and the construction of industrial CVD equipment (ATL) and a SME expert in development of novel monitoring hardware and software solutions (IRIS). 1.3 Concept and approach  
(a) Overall concept underpinning DECOAT  
The playing field. Increasing recycling is crucial, especially for plastics and textiles, as illustrated by the recently published European strategy on plastics6 or the voluntary engagement of the plastics industry to recycle 50% by 20407. However large the need and however impressive the targets, today only 6% of new plastic materials used stems from recycling8. Also within the textile industry the use of recycled material is limited: for clothing only about 1% is closed loop, ca 12% cascade recycling3. A great deal of work is underway to improve this (also via European projects), focussing on technical, economic and logistical aspects. As a result, we assume in the near future well-established networks throughout Europe where waste is collected and brought to local recycling facilities. At first, this network will deal with the current generation of products and materials that are not made for recycling. Consequently, only a small fraction of all materials is being recycled because the cost to the yield and quality ratio is too high compared with the one of virgin materials. Therefore, the next steps are products designed for recycling and made of intrinsically recyclable materials. This concept is being developed, e.g. for high added value applications like aerospace. However, for vast application areas this principle is not very advanced yet.  
The identified need. We will implement a solution for a specific class of hard to recycle items: textiles and plastics parts with (multilayer) ‘coatings’. For plastics, these can be found eg in automotive, construction and electronics consumer goods. For textiles, typical uses are for technical textiles (outdoor gear, protective clothing, automotive applications and construction) and interior textiles (e.g. upholstery, mattress covers or curtains). The coating can be added for performance or aesthetics reasons. Coating, paints or adhesives have to possess excellent adhesion because of the durability of the coated product, and as such cannot easily be removed. Consequently, full objects are discarded, even though the coating often only comprises some % of the total weight. With DECOAT we will reduce the amount of waste material to the specific adhesive. This holds for end-of-life products but it is also one of the types of production scraps that are not efficiently recycled yet. Within this larger topic of debonding an emerging specific challenge is how to deal with scrap and end-of-life parts containing integrated electronics (from smart textiles, smart plastics and wearables).  
DECOAT proposed solution: enable the recycling of coated parts, which refers to a two-level approach: (i) ‘disassembly’ is separating the connections among the different physical parts; (ii) ‘debonding’ or ‘removal’ refers to the removal of coatings or tie layers from the fabric or plastics object. This 2-step approach is illustrated in the figures for a backpack. These two steps can be performed separately (or simultaneously in some cases) during the recycling process. DECOAT triggerable separations mechanisms will be suited for both steps and fill the missing gap for circular use of coated parts (see sketch in section 1.1). Within DECOAT, we will focus mostly on the 2nd level, i.e. the removal of coatings. But, the same principle of triggered debonding enables the disassembly step. So, DECOAT developments are relevant for both steps and we will refer to the processes as ‘debonding’.  
Interdisciplinary aspects: DECOAT combines expertise and knowledge from several disciplines: polymer chemistry, physics, coatings and paints, textiles and plastics processing, recycling, design, and environmental science. This is needed and allows us to tackle the interdisciplinary issues required for reaching circularity. One such key issue is to tune the existing knowledge on triggerable materials to industrial requirements for coated textiles and plastics.  
Stakeholder involvement: interaction with the stakeholders is deemed crucial, especially with the waste actors. Therefore, we have added ISWA (NL) as a partner, a worldwide association of solid waste actors that promote sustainable waste management and perform dissemination and policy work. For further strong interaction with a wide group of stakeholders, we have established the DECOAT Advisory and Support Group (ASG), containing 17 members from coating and recycling companies, industry associations, a public authority, standardisation bodies and a publisher, see also section 3.2.1.1f and section 4 for a detailed description.

1.3 Concept and methodology  
1.3.1. PHOTONFOOD concept  

A large number of studies have demonstrated that MIR spectroscopy in combination with advanced data analysis has the capability to identify and detect a broad range of microorganisms and chemical contaminants in a lab setting15,16. Since its invention, our interest from industries in this technique has grown considerably. The technology can be used for identification of contaminants at the genus, species level and in

most cases even down to strain level. For bacteria and, recently for fungi, it has been shown that identification is possible with an accuracy above 90% on species level. For moulds, MIR spectroscopy outcompetes any existing technology in accuracy and the broad range of fungi that can be addressed16,17. However, state-of-the-art applications are performed in environments and under conditions that are not relevant for on-site use along the farm-to-fork food production chain16,17. State-of-the-art sample preparation protocols for detecting and identifying microbial contamination by MIR analysis are based on culturing and sub-culturing whereas spectra are obtained in pure culture 16,17.  
The detection of mycotoxins by MIR spectroscopy has been demonstrated by us in the spectral range of 1500–1800 cm -1 in extracts from grains (see Fig. 2). However, the challenge is the exceedingly low limits of detection that are required from a legislation point of view. In addition, the potential presence of multiple mycotoxins and contaminants makes the situation even more difficult. The low concentrations of mycotoxin and other contaminants such as antibiotics and pesticides make the direct detection and separation of signals in food matrices impossible. To date, this challenge can only be met by using sophisticated analytical techniques in especially equipped laboratory (e.g., LC-MS/MS) or via antibody-based immunoassays (e.g. ELISA).  

The PHOTONFOOD concept is to perform preconcentration of samples using paper-based microfluidics strategies and to combine the cheap and environmentally friendly paper-based microfluidics with label-free MIR photonic read-out devices (see Fig. 3).  

While paper microfluidic tests for numerous applications in point-of-care testing, and environmental research have been demonstrated with detection limits in the mid ppb range1718, few of these offer an integrated solution that also addresses sampling and sample pretreatment. 1.3 Concept and methodology  
Concept  
We are a 4-year RIA project with 17 partners from 8 countries across Europe (Belgium, Italy, Spain, Germany, Portugal, Ireland, Luxembourg, and the UK). We are a very strong consortium with plastic suppliers companies, innovative biotechnology and bioplastics companies, and RTDs, as well as large end users. Our proposal aims to develop biotech solutions for AWP treatment that will include plastic-degrading microorganisms, novel enzymes, insect larvae, and earthworms, which performance will be demonstrated in real plastic contaminated matrix both in ex-situ (composting, vermicomposting, reactor, and insect rearing chambers) and in-situ (soil) experiments. In addition to compost and vermicompost, we will obtain a portfolio of added value products including bioplastic formulations for application in agriculture (mulching films, sticks, pots), food packaging (trays, rigid containers, films), and coatings based on chitin/chitosan extracted from insects rearing on AWP; biofertilizers from organic matter leftovers after AWP treatments and chitin extraction. We will dedicate great attention to establishing simple and reproducible conditions that can be replicated on farms or municipalities as a profitable activity that can benefit the whole value chain from plastic producers to the end-users involved in our project.  
While modern economies highly depend on plastics in Europe, in fact, we consume in the range of 50 Mtons per year, only 25-31% of the plastic waste in Europe is currently recycled, and the larger share is incinerated or even landfilled (high variability between countries). In light of the EU’s policy objective of achieving a resource-efficient recycling society, it is hard to accept that in Europe we still landfill nearly 40-50% of plastic waste. This all contributes to the depletion of limited resources and to the pollution of the environment. Besides those often non-optimal disposal routes, plastics, in particular packaging (bags, bottles, lids, etc.), account for 89-95% or roughly 8 Mtons of marine litter, and 17 Mtons of plastic packaging is irreversibly dispersed on land every year worldwide. Thus, the very slow rate of degradation of plastics leads to accumulating pollution persisting for hundreds of years. Microbial biotechnology may contribute to this enormous challenge. The advances in our understanding of microbial functions from enzyme to pathways, and entire metabolic networks now allow us to engineer complex metabolic functions in microbes. With the ever-advancing tools from synthetic biology and the implementation of new strategies involving the use of microbial, insect, and earthworm consortia, we can overcome the major challenges for plastic waste bioremediation and provide simultaneously a biotechnological plastic waste-based value chain for the synthesis of added value products, which will empower the recycling industry to a qualitatively new dimension. Proving biotech solutions for plastics EoL in our target sectors is of greatest relevance as they consume more than 40% of all plastic produced each year (packaging 40%, agriculture 4%). As such, we need to invest more in research and development and find more cost-competitive solutions to allow their eventual large-scale market-driven uptake.  

Our project will:  
- Develop a range of biotech tools with capabilities for degrading plastics wastes from agriculture and food packaging  
- Validate the use of the resulting biotech solutions for the bioremediation of soil and organic matrices contaminated with plastic and for the biotransformation of plastics into added value products  
- Progress in the use of the by-products from the process (chitin, chitosan, and organic leftovers) for the production of agricultural and packaging items and biofertilizers  
- Assess at each process step, the environmental impacts and economic feasibility in comparison with existing benchmarks.  
- Provide recommendations for suitable business approaches for the new process and resulting added value products from an integrated bio-based value chain perspective

In RECOVER, our trans-disciplinary team combines all the relevant skills and organisations needed to address the full extent of the challenge of developing and validating innovative sustainable biotech solutions for processing plastic waste into added value products. The participation of a number of leading industry players directly and through the Advisory Board (AB) proves the actual soundness of the concept to generate large impact and will maximize our consortium capability in transferring this know-how to enable post-project further demonstration and qualification into broadly available market-ready solutions. Furthermore, the participating associations/NGOs, covering waste management and farmers mainly, will also help raise environmental awareness and policy outreach that could further encourage the adoption of our new respectively ex and in situ solutions.  
To complement our consortium, further stakeholders will be involved in an AB.
